findings.reachability: Reachable by any caller passing attacker-influenced $name (e.g., a setting key read from DB or URL param); rendered in admin UI HTML
findings.justification: The $name parameter is interpolated verbatim into id=, for=, name=, and value= HTML attributes without esc_attr(), so a malicious value breaks out of the attribute context and injects arbitrary HTML/JS.

findings.reachability: Any caller passing $options array with attacker-influenced 'val' or 'label' entries (e.g., values read from DB/user input)
findings.justification: $tmp['val'] and $tmp['label'] are interpolated directly into <option> tag attribute and content positions without esc_attr()/esc_html(), allowing attribute breakout and HTML injection.

findings.reachability: Any caller passing $name or $options with attacker-influenced 'val' or 'label' entries
findings.justification: $name, $tmp['val'], and $tmp['label'] are interpolated into id=, name=, value= attributes and label text without any escaping, enabling attribute injection or HTML injection.

findings.reachability: Any HTTP request; $browser_name, $browser_version[0], and $os are derived from HTTP_* request headers via $_SERVER and flow into the return string which is stored/displayed in logs
findings.justification: $browser_name, $browser_version[0], and $os originate from HTTP request headers and are concatenated into an HTML string without esc_html(), enabling XSS when the result is rendered in the activity log UI.

findings.reachability: All HTML output from this file passes through wp_kses_wf(), which is intended as the XSS sanitizer; exploitable by any input that ends up in HTML rendered via this function
findings.justification: The custom wp_kses allowlist explicitly whitelists the <script> tag (with src= and type= attributes), meaning any attacker-controlled HTML reaching wp_kses_wf() can include arbitrary script tags that wp_kses will pass through unstripped.

findings.reachability: Same as above — all HTML output routed through wp_kses_wf()
findings.justification: The allowlist also whitelists <style> tags, allowing injected CSS (e.g., expression()-based attacks in older IE, or CSS data exfiltration) to pass through the kses sanitizer.

findings.reachability: Any request triggering IP logging; REMOTE_ADDR is attacker-controlled in some proxy/load-balancer configurations
findings.justification: sanitize_url() is applied to an IP address — it is not an IP validator and does not strip all invalid characters — so a non-standard REMOTE_ADDR value passes through and is stored in logs, potentially causing second-order issues if the IP is later used in SQL or HTML contexts.
